one_line_takeaway: By decoupling reconnaissance from exploitation via ground-truth vulnerability injection, the paper shows LLM pentest agents can hit up to 90% exploit success once told what's vulnerable, but autonomous reconnaissance alone plateaus near 50%, meaning conventional end-to-end benchmarks substantially undercount true exploitation capability due to reconnaissance-stage masking.
key_finding: With ground-truth vulnerability context injected (VEKB, aligned 50-CVE subset, Gemini 3 Pro), Hexstrike-AI reaches FSR 90.0% (45/50) and TVR 90.0% (45/50); CyberstrikeAI reaches FSR 80.0% (40/50) with TVR 86.0% (43/50); RedAmon FSR 74.0% (37/50), TVR 86.0% (43/50); Decepticon FSR 70.0% (35/50), TVR 88.0% (44/50); NeuroSploit FSR 62.0% (31/50), TVR 84.0% (42/50). Without any knowledge base, autonomous TVR collapses to roughly 48.0-58.0% across frameworks (e.g. Decepticon 48.0%, Hexstrike-AI 54.0%), while FSR without KB is comparatively higher (48.0-66.0%), producing an inversion the authors call a masking effect: on the zero-knowledge baseline they observe 51 'masking instances' where reconnaissance failed but the agent still succeeded at exploitation once ground truth was injected -- direct evidence that E2E evaluation undercounts real exploitation capability. In the 70-testbed core ablation, VEKB integration raised Hexstrike-AI/Gemini-3-Pro FSR from 61.4% (without KB) to 81.4% (with VEKB), and larger/more capable base models did not monotonically help: swapping Gemini 3 Pro for GPT-5 in the monolithic CyberStrikeAI framework dropped FSR from 77.1% to 64.3%, attributed to GPT-5's greater susceptibility to 'attention dispersion' on verbose error telemetry when the architecture lacks state isolation, whereas the decoupled multi-agent (MCP-isolated) architecture stayed robust across both base models. Architecturally, the paper reports distinct capability niches: multi-agent/MCP-isolated designs (Hexstrike-AI) do best on long-sequence interactions like deserialization; monolithic designs (CyberStrikeAI) do best on short-chain injection flaws; graph-driven designs (RedAmon, Decepticon) do best on cross-session access-control/IDOR-style business-logic flaws; constrained symbolic-tree agents (NeuroSploit) are limited to deterministic N-day PoC reproduction due to restricted payload mutation.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a two-stage 'decoupled' evaluation framework for LLM-based web penetration-testing agents that separates the reconnaissance phase (finding/identifying a vulnerability, e.g. producing the correct CVE identifier) from the exploitation phase (payload construction and exploit execution). When an agent fails autonomous reconnaissance, the framework performs 'ground-truth injection': it pauses execution and injects the correct vulnerability identifier/context into the prompt before the exploitation phase, breaking the error-cascading chain that the authors argue plagues conventional end-to-end (E2E) black-box pentest evaluations. The authors also introduce a 'Verified Empirical Knowledge Base' (VEKB) ablation (zero-knowledge vs. knowledge-augmented, i.e. with vs. without standardized PoC templates) to separately measure factual/knowledge deficits from architectural/reasoning deficits. They built a 70-testbed benchmark of web vulnerabilities (reconstructed from Vulhub and Vulfocus, mapped to CWE/OWASP Top 10 categories) and used it to benchmark five open-source pentest agent frameworks (Hexstrike-AI, CyberStrikeAI, RedAmon, NeuroSploit, Decepticon) covering MCP-driven multi-agent, monolithic, graph-driven, and constrained/symbolic architectures, defining two metrics: Targeted Vulnerability Recall (TVR, reconnaissance) and Functional Success Rate (FSR, exploitation after ground-truth injection).
threat_model: Offensive/red-team automation threat model: the 'attacker' is an LLM-driven pentesting agent operating in an isolated sandbox (Kali Linux attack node against 70 independently deployed, disposable Docker victim containers). The paper does not evaluate defenses; it measures the offensive capability boundary of current LLM agents against known (disclosed-CVE) web vulnerabilities across CWE classes: CWE-74 injection (SQLi, OS command injection, SSTI; 27/70, 38.6%), CWE-284 broken access control (IDOR, Redis/Docker unauthorized access; 13/70, 18.6%), CWE-22 path traversal (arbitrary file read, insecure file upload; 13/70, 18.6%), CWE-502 deserialization (Weblogic deserialization, Fastjson RCE; 12/70, 17.1%), and CWE-16 configuration/business-logic issues (5/70, 7.1%). No live/production targets, no WAF-evasion or active-defense (EDR) interaction is modeled -- the authors explicitly flag this as a limitation, and the benchmark relies on disclosed CVEs rather than zero-days.
limitations: Limitations stated by the authors themselves: (1) the benchmark is built entirely on disclosed CVEs with standardized PoC templates, so ground-truth injection mainly measures reproduction of known exploits rather than genuine zero-day exploit-generation capability -- they propose future work injecting only endpoint/parameter/CWE-category (not the CVE id) to better isolate zero-day capability; (2) the isolated sandbox does not model dynamic interaction with active defenses (e.g. EDR), so robustness to evasion/detection is untested. Not stated but evident from the text: the five evaluated frameworks (Hexstrike-AI, CyberStrikeAI, RedAmon, NeuroSploit, Decepticon) are open-source pentest agents the paper treats as given baselines -- no code/reproducibility artifact link is given in the paper, and the 'Verified Empirical Knowledge Base' construction process itself is not described in the excerpted methodology beyond 'standardized PoC templates'.

datasets_used: 70 high-fidelity web vulnerability testbeds reconstructed from Vulhub and Vulfocus, mapped to OWASP Top 10 / CWE categories (CWE-74 injection 27/70=38.6%, CWE-284 broken access control 13/70=18.6%, CWE-22 path traversal 13/70=18.6%, CWE-502 deserialization 12/70=17.1%, CWE-16 config/business-logic 5/70=7.1%)
Aligned 50-vulnerability subset of the 70-testbed set used for cross-framework generalization comparison
quantitative_results: Hexstrike-AI (Gemini 3 Pro), 70-testbed ablation: with VEKB TVR 61(87.1%), FSR 57(81.4%); without KB TVR 38(54.3%), FSR 43(61.4%)
Hexstrike-AI (GPT-5 High), 70-testbed ablation: with VEKB TVR 56(80.0%), FSR 54(77.1%); without KB TVR 33(47.1%), FSR 38(54.3%)
CyberstrikeAI (Gemini 3 Pro), 70-testbed ablation: with VEKB TVR 59(84.3%), FSR 54(77.1%); without KB TVR 43(61.4%), FSR 39(55.7%)
CyberstrikeAI (GPT-5 High), 70-testbed ablation: with VEKB TVR 61(87.1%), FSR 45(64.3%); without KB TVR 32(45.7%), FSR 31(44.3%)

50-CVE aligned subset, Gemini 3 Pro, with VEKB (TVR/FSR): Hexstrike-AI 45(90.0%)/45(90.0%); CyberstrikeAI 43(86.0%)/40(80.0%); RedAmon 43(86.0%)/37(74.0%); Decepticon 44(88.0%)/35(70.0%); NeuroSploit 42(84.0%)/31(62.0%)

50-CVE aligned subset, Gemini 3 Pro, without KB (TVR/FSR): Hexstrike-AI 27(54.0%)/33(66.0%); CyberstrikeAI 29(58.0%)/29(58.0%); RedAmon 28(56.0%)/29(58.0%); Decepticon 24(48.0%)/24(48.0%); NeuroSploit 26(52.0%)/27(54.0%)
51 'masking instances' observed under the zero-knowledge baseline (reconnaissance failure followed by successful exploitation after ground-truth injection)
baselines_compared: Hexstrike-AI (MCP-driven multi-agent system, isolated MCP server, tool-augmented)
CyberStrikeAI (monolithic, task delegation, shared context space, OWASP repository knowledge interface)
RedAmon (graph-driven multi-agent, fan-out/fan-in, knowledge graph, PTY & OSINT tools)
NeuroSploit (constrained agent, symbolic attack-tree reasoning, containerized state, prompt guards)
Decepticon (graph-driven multi-agent, collaborative workflow, global topology state, auto-toolchain)
Base foundation models compared: GPT-5 High vs. Gemini 3 Pro
